coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen, phosphorous co-doped carbon quantum dots as glucose and lactate sensor
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Yashwanth, H.J.
Naik, M. Madhukara
Dileep, M.S.
Muniyappa, Murthy
Navya Rani, M.
coredata.dc:description: The present work emphases on the development of a sensor constructed based on the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doped with heteroatoms such as nitrogen and phosphorous (NPCQDs) by one step microwave radiation assisted method. Cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry are used to investigate and compare the sensing performance of CQDs, nitrogen doped CQDs (NCQDs), phosphorous doped CQDs (CQDs) and NPCQDs towards glucose and lactate. It is observed that NPCQDs shows enhanced current response compared to CQDs, NCQDs and PCQDs due to the injection of extra electrons by dopants. Further, NPCQDs sensor exhibited good stability over 5000 s which is excellent in its class.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Doping
Nitrogen
Phosphorous
Sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectra and (b) Raman spectra of CQDs, NCQDs, PCQDs and NPCQDs.

(a) CV curves of NPCQDs in 0.1 M NaOH electrolyte for different glucose concentration, (b) CV curves of different samples at 5 mM glucose concentration, (c) plot of anodic current versus glucose concentration, (d) CV curves of NPCQDs in 0.2 M NaOH electrolyte for different lactic acid concentration (e) CV curves of different samples at 15 mM lactate concentration, (f) plot of anodic current versus lactic acid concentration.

Chronoamperometric response of NPCQD modified Ni foam electrode in 0.1 M NaOH (a) for successive addition of 0.5 M glucose concentration for every 200 s, (b) for successive addition of 0.5 M lactic acid concentration for every 200 s, stability check for (c) 5 mM glucose and (d) 5 mM lactate.
The schematic illustration of preparation of NPCQDs by microwave radiation assisted method.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen, phosphorous co-doped carbon quantum dots as glucose and lactate sensor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work emphases on the development of a sensor constructed based on the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doped with heteroatoms such as nitrogen and phosphorous (NPCQDs) by one step microwave radiation assisted method. Cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry are used to investigate and compare the sensing performance of CQDs, nitrogen doped CQDs (NCQDs), phosphorous doped CQDs (CQDs) and NPCQDs towards glucose and lactate. It is observed that NPCQDs shows enhanced current response compared to CQDs, NCQDs and PCQDs due to the injection of extra electrons by dopants. Further, NPCQDs sensor exhibited good stability over 5000 s which is excellent in its class.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the multifunctional materials have gained much attention over the past few years in the field of research due to their tremendous applications such as energy storage, light emission, energy conversion, catalyst and sensor [1]. Among different kinds of multifunctional materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), whose size lies less than 10 nm have got attention due to their tunable fluorescence, lesser bandgap, low toxicity, superior optical property, enhanced electrical property etc., compared to semiconductor quantum dots [2]. These properties of CQDs makes it multifunctional in various application such as sensing, supercapacitors, drug delivery, bioimaging etc [2]. Further, the CQDs properties can be enhanced by doping with nitrogen [3], phosphorous [4], boron [5], sulfur [6] etc., in which the dopant atom bonds with the CQDs through the surface functional groups, hence stimulating their properties compared to CQDs alone. Doping introduces localized states within the band gap of CQDs, which can lower the energy required for electronic transitions. This can improve the sensitivity of sensors by allowing them to respond to lower concentrations of analytes [4]. Heteroatoms like nitrogen can create donor or acceptor levels, enhancing the charge carrier mobility and altering the conductivity of CQDs [5]. This leads to better electrochemical responses. Nitrogen and phosphorus introduce functional groups that serve as active sites for chemical interactions. This increases the reactivity of CQDs and improves their ability to adsorb target analytes, which is critical for sensing applications [6]. Also, the introduction of heteroatoms N and P in CQDs can increase the hydrophilicity of CQDs, enhancing their dispersion in aqueous solutions and improving their interaction with analytes in sensing applications [7]. CQDs often exhibit enhanced thermal and chemical stability, which is crucial for reliable performance in real-world sensing applications where conditions may vary [8]. The functional groups created by heteroatoms N and P doping can lead to selective interactions with specific analytes, enhancing the selectivity of the sensors [8]. Overall, nitrogen and phosphorus doping significantly influences the electrochemical properties of CQDs compared to undoped CQDs, improving their performance in sensing applications [9]. The modifications in electronic structure, surface properties, and stability make doped CQDs a promising material for various sensing technologies, including environmental monitoring, biomedical diagnostics, and food safety [10].

Recently, researchers have even tried to co-dope CQDs by nitrogen-phosphor [11], nitrogen-boron [12], nitrogen-sulfur [13] etc. to enhance the performance of doped CQDs. In this context, Bao et al. [14], have reported the sensing of elemental Fe3+ using N, P co-doped CQDs, finding of dopamine using a nitrogen boron co-doped CQDs was reported by Dadkhah et al. [15], Song et al. [16], have used N, S co-doped CQDs for the selective detection of glutathione. In our previous studies, we synthesized nitrogen-phosphorous co-doped CQDs by microwave radiation assisted method and, observed the enhancement in photocatalytic activity of NPCQDs compared to nitrogen doped CQDs, phosphorous doped CQDs and CQDs alone due to the extra electrons injected from nitrogen and phosphorous dopants [17]. These extra electrons by the dopants may increases the conductivity in NPCQDs, which can be utilized to enhance its electrochemical sensing performance.

Therefore, herein, we developed nitrogen, phosphorous co-doped carbon quantum dots and, applied it as glucose and lactate detecting electrochemical sensor. The significance of glucose sensing finds application in food monitoring, pharmaceutical analysis and blood glucose testing [18]. Similarly, lactate sensing find significance in the field of food technology, agro technology, biotechnology and bioprocessing [19].

The nitrogen and phosphorous were co-doped in carbon quantum dots by one-step microwave radiation method and the procedure is explained in detail elsewhere [17]. 0.1 M of glucose, 6 mM of ethylenediamine and 2 mM of phosphoric acid were mixed in 20 mL of double distilled water (DDW) and stirred for 2 h. Then, it was kept in a microwave oven of 800 W for 5 min. Than the obtained solution is centrifused centrifuged (REMI R–4C) at 12,000 RPM for 15 min and supernatant was collected. Further, it was purified using 1 kda dialysis membrane under constant stirring against DDW for 3 days by changing the DDW for every 24 h. The resulting dialyzed solution was freeze-dried to get NPCQD.

Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was carried out using JASCO FTIR-6100 spectrometer. Raman spectra was measured using Renishaw Invia laser Raman microscope. The electrochemical experiments were carried out using K-Lyte electrochemical workstation. The set-up designed for the electrochemical study of glucose as well as lactate sensing consisted of three electrodes, namely the working electrode comprised of NPCQDs modified Ni foam, platinum wire as the counter electrode and the Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode in 0.1 M NaOH electrolyte solution. Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) was carried out in the potential from 0.0 to 0.55 V with a scan rate of 25 mV/s. Chronoamperometry studies were performed at a constant voltage of 0.55 V with the addition of glucose/lactate for every 200 s. The stability of the developed sensor was performed over 5000 s continuously and the current response was observed.

In our previous publication, it was reported that the synthesized NPCQDs were found to be non-agglomerating, spherical shaped having size of (2–5) nm and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic results revealed the doping of nitrogen and phosphourus in carbon quantum dots (Yashwanth et al., 2020). The schematic representation of synthesis of nitrogen, phosphourus co-doped CQDs in shown in Scheme 1 . When the precursor, glucose was exposed to microwave radiation, it undergo polymerization followed by carbonization and finally forms the CQDs (Bhattacharyya et al., 2017). During this process, the nitrogen from ethylenediamine and phosphourus from orthophosphoric acid were also doped to CQDs resulting in the formation of nitrogen, phosphourus co-doped carbon quantum dots.

Fig. 1 (a) shows FTIR spectrum of CQD, NCQD, PCQD and NPCQD. The peak observed around 3100 cm−1 – 3600 cm−1 in all the spectra which is ascribed to –OH stretching vibrations of amino and hydroxyl groups [20]. The peaks obtained for NCQDs around 2857 cm−1, 1566 cm−1 and 1408 cm−1 respectively corresponding to C–H, CN, –COOH stretching. The peak appeared at 3100 cm−1 – 3600 cm−1 of NCQDs suggests that nitrogen is successfully doped into CQD [3]. Similarly, the peaks for PCQD sample were observed around 1630 cm−1, 1148 cm−1 and 859 cm−1 corresponding to CO, PO and P–O–C stretching modes respectively [4]. The presence of these functional groups confirms the successful doping of nitrogen and phosphorous into their respective CQDs. These all peaks were observed in NPCQDs indicating the presence of nitrogen and phosphorous functional groups in NPCQDs.

The Raman spectrum of CQDs, NCQDs, PCQDs and NPCQDs is shown in Fig. 1(b). All the Raman spectra exhibited the peak at 1586 cm−1 attributed to G band, It appears as a result of the E2g mode's first order scattering in carbon materials [20]. The other peak is detected at 1362 cm−1 attributed to D band which attributed to the presence of defects in CQDs [7]. It is evident that the intensity of D band increased for NCQDs, PCQDs and NPCQDs compared to CQDs due to the formation of huge number of small sp2 domains. In addition to this, a blue shift was observed for G band from 1570 cm−1–1585 cm−1 indicating the enhanced disorderness which may be due to doping of nitrogen and phosphorous atoms.

Cyclic voltammetry was used to explore the electrochemical performance of NPCQDs towards glucose as well as lactate sensing and is as shown in Fig. 2 . The CV curves bare Ni foam and NPCQDs modified Ni foam in the absence and presence of glucose at the sweep rate of 25 mVs-1 is shown in Fig. 2(a). It is evident from Fig. 2(a) that neither anodic nor cathodic peaks appeared for bare Ni foam whereas NPCQDs modified Ni foam showed both reduction as well as oxidation peak in the CV curves. It can also be noticed that the anodic current increased wth increased glucose concentrtaion representing the good electrochemical sensing performance of NPCQDs. Further, the cyclic voltametry performance of NPCQDs was compared with that of PCQDs, NCQDs, CQDs and is shown in Fig. 2(b) at 5 mM. It is noteworthy to observe that the anodic current increased in the order NPCQDs > PCQDs > NCQDs > CQDs. The greater current response of NPCQDs is due to enhanced number of sp2 domains which is supported by Raman spectroscopic results. This large number of small sp2 domains in NPCQDs facilitates the ion and electrons transports thereby showing improved glucose sensing. Further, in our previous study, we reported that the work function of NPCQDs decreased more compared to that of PCQDs, NCQDs and CQDs due to the injection of extra electrons into CQDs by nitrogen and phosphorous dopants thereby creating the extra energy states [17]. These extra electrons from the nitrogen and phosphorous dopants in CQDs increases the conductivity of NPCQDs thereby enhancing its sensing performance as follows [18].
(1)
N PCQDs → NPCQD
s ∗
+
e −
(2)
N PCQD
s ∗
+ glu cos
e → NPCQDs + glucolactone

Futher, the anodic current response along with the varying glucose concentration was obtained as depicted in Fig. 2(c). The current increased lineraly with gluocose concentrtaion for NPCQDs as well as PCQDs, NCQDs and CQDs. Nevertheless, NPCQDs showed higher current response compared to other quantum dots due to the extra electrons provided by dopants as explained in above section, which results in good electrochemical sensing performance. The sensitivity of NPCQDs for glucose sensing was found to be 0.147 mAμM−1cm−2.

The CV curves of NPCQDs for various concentration of lactate at the sweep rate of 15 mVs−1 in 0.1 M NaOH electrolyte is shown in Fig. 2(d). It can be seen from figure that anodic current increased with increase in lactate concentrtaion revealing its good electrochemical sensing performance towards lactate. The lactate sensing of NPCQDs was compared with that of PCQDs, NCQDs and CQDs at 15 mM lactate, and is shown in Fig. 2(e). It is noteworthy to observe that NPCQDs showed enhanced anodic current among all due to large number of small sp2 domains as well as extra electrons injected by nitrogen and phosphorus dopants as explained in above section. The mechanism of lactate sensing by NPCQDs is as follows [21].
(3)
N PCQDs → NPCQD
s ∗
+
e −
(4)
N PCQDs ∗ + lactate → NPCQDs + pyruvate

The anodic current response along with the varying lactate concentration is shown in Fig. 2(f), which depicted linear trend for all sensors. The sensitivity of NPCQDs sensor for lactate was found to be 0.108 mAμM−1cm−2.

The chronoamperometric response of NPCQDs for glucose and lactate are shown in Fig. 3 (a) and (b) respectively. The response curve was recorded by increasing glucose and lactate concentration for every 200 s. It is clear that the current increased step wise for both glucose as well as for lactate addition. Nevertheless, the NPCQDs exhibited faster response for glucose compared to lactate.

The stability of the developed sensor over 5000 s for both glucose as well as lactate sensing was studied and is shown in Fig. 3(c) and (d) respectively. It can be noticed from figure that NPCQDs sensor depicted good stability of 95 % for glucose sensing at the end of 5000 s. Nonetheless, the stability of NPCQDs was decreased to 80 % for lactate sening at the end of 5000 s.

Nitrogen and phosphorous co-doped carbon quantum dots (NPCQDs) were successfully developed for the electrochemical sensing of glucose and lactate. The NPCQDs sensor exhibited better performance for glucose and lactate sensing compared to PCQDs, NCQDs, and CQDs due to the formation of large number of small sp2 domains and injection of extra electrons from dopants to CQDs. These better electrochemical performance for glucose and lactate sensing indicates that NPCQDs based sensor can be more affordable, reliable they often exhibit good photostability, which is crucial for maintaining performance over time, particularly in real-time monitoring applications, NPCQDs can be integrated with various platforms, including flexible electronics and microfluidic devices, enhancing their applicability in diverse field. These advantages position NPCQDs as promising candidates for a variety of sensing, Wearable Technology, Environmental Monitoring, and bio medical applications, potentially surpassing the performance of some existing sensor technologies.

H.J. Yashwanth: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. M. Madhukara Naik: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation. M.S. Dileep: Resources. Murthy Muniyappa: Validation. M. Navya Rani: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
